q0.question: What is an ATS?
q0.answer: An Applicant Tracking System (ATS) is software used by employers to filter and rank resumes before human review. Over 98% of Fortune 500 companies use ATS.

q1.question: How do I know if my resume is ATS-friendly?
q1.answer: Our resume builder automatically checks your resume for ATS compatibility and provides a score with improvement suggestions.

q2.answer: Both PDF and .docx are accepted by modern ATS. Our builder exports to both formats.

q0.answer: Focus on your education, academic projects, extracurricular activities, volunteering, and any part-time jobs. These all demonstrate valuable skills to employers.

q1.answer: Include your GPA if it's 3.0 or above. For recent graduates, a strong GPA can compensate for limited work experience.

q2.answer: Keep it to one page. As a student, you don't have enough experience to justify a longer resume, and recruiters prefer concise documents.

q3.answer: Include technical skills (software, programming languages), language skills, and soft skills like communication, teamwork, and project management.

faq.title: Chronological Resume FAQ
faq.subtitle: Questions about the chronological resume format.

q0.question: What is a chronological resume?
q0.answer: A chronological (or reverse-chronological) resume lists your work experience starting with your most recent job, working backwards. It's the most widely used and accepted format.

q1.question: Who should use a chronological resume format?
q1.answer: Anyone with a consistent work history and no major gaps. It's the best choice for most professionals in any industry.

q2.question: When should I NOT use a chronological format?
q2.answer: If you have significant employment gaps, are changing careers, or have very limited experience. In those cases, a functional or hybrid format may work better.

q3.question: Is a chronological resume better for ATS?
q3.answer: Yes. The reverse-chronological format is the most reliably parsed by applicant tracking systems.

q4.question: How far back should I go on a chronological resume?
q4.answer: Generally 10-15 years is sufficient for most professionals. Older experience can be summarized or omitted unless it's specifically relevant.

section3.title: Functional Resume Tips
section3.tip0: Group your skills into 3-4 competency categories (e.g., Leadership, Technical Skills, Communication) with bullet points under each.
section3.tip1: Still include a brief work history section at the bottom — omitting it entirely raises red flags for ATS and recruiters.
section3.tip2: Lead with a strong professional summary that bridges your past experience and target role.
section3.tip3: Quantify achievements within skill categories: 'Leadership: Managed volunteer teams of 10+ across 3 community events'.
section3.tip4: Use the hybrid/combination format if possible — it balances skills emphasis with a recognizable chronological section.

faq.title: Functional Resume FAQ
faq.subtitle: Questions about the functional resume format.

q0.question: What is a functional resume?
q0.answer: A functional resume organizes your content by skills and competencies rather than work history. It de-emphasizes job titles and dates in favor of what you can do.

q1.question: Do recruiters like functional resumes?
q1.answer: Many recruiters are skeptical of functional resumes as they can seem to hide career gaps or lack of experience. Use a hybrid format when possible.

q2.question: Is a functional resume ATS-friendly?
q2.answer: Less so than chronological resumes. ATS systems are optimized for work history sections. Including a brief work history still helps with ATS parsing.

q3.question: When should I use a functional resume?
q3.answer: When changing careers, returning from a long gap, or when your skills are more relevant than your specific job titles and employers.

q4.question: What's the difference between functional and hybrid resume formats?
q4.answer: A hybrid (combination) resume leads with skills but also includes a full chronological work history. It's generally preferred over a pure functional format.

finalCta.title: Lead with What You Can Do
finalCta.description: Build a functional resume that highlights your strongest skills and reframes your experience for any role.

section3.title: One-Page Resume Tips
section3.tip0: Cut anything that doesn't directly support your case for the role. No filler, no irrelevant experience.
section3.tip1: Use 10-11pt body font and tight but readable line spacing to maximize content density.
section3.tip2: Combine short tenures under one employer entry if they were internal promotions or role changes.
section3.tip3: Use concise bullet points — 1-2 lines maximum per bullet, starting with a strong action verb.
section3.tip4: Shrink margins slightly (0.5 inch instead of 1 inch) to reclaim space without sacrificing readability.

faq.title: One-Page Resume FAQ
faq.subtitle: Questions about keeping your resume to one page.

q0.question: Is a one-page resume really necessary?
q0.answer: For most professionals with under 10 years of experience, yes. One page demonstrates conciseness and respect for the recruiter's time.

q1.question: Can a senior professional use a one-page resume?
q1.answer: It's impressive if done well, but two pages are acceptable for senior professionals with 15+ years of experience. Quality over quantity always.

q2.question: What should I cut to make my resume fit one page?
q2.answer: Remove: jobs older than 10 years, unrelated positions, references section, high school education (if you have a degree), and any filler responsibilities.

q3.question: Should I use a smaller font to fit everything on one page?
q3.answer: Only down to 10pt for body text. Below that, readability suffers. Instead, trim content and adjust margins before shrinking font size.

q4.question: Is a one-page resume better for ATS?
q4.answer: ATS doesn't care about page count, but single-page resumes typically have less formatting complexity, which reduces parsing errors.

faq.title: Resume Format 2025 FAQ
faq.subtitle: Your questions about modern resume formats answered.

q0.question: What is the best resume format in 2025?
q0.answer: The reverse-chronological format remains the gold standard. The hybrid (combination) format is increasingly popular for mid-career and senior professionals.

q1.question: Should I use a one-column or two-column resume in 2025?
q1.answer: One-column remains safest for ATS. Some modern ATS systems now handle two-column layouts, but one-column guarantees compatibility across all platforms.

q2.question: Are visual or graphic resumes a good idea in 2025?
q2.answer: For creative roles and human audiences, potentially yes. For ATS-heavy processes, visual elements remain risky. Have both versions ready.

q3.question: Should I include a photo on my resume in 2025?
q3.answer: In the US and UK, no. In France, Germany, and many European countries, a professional headshot remains standard.

q4.question: How is AI changing resume screening in 2025?
q4.answer: Many companies now use AI to pre-screen resumes before a human ever sees them. Keyword optimization, clear formatting, and tailored content are more important than ever.

q0.question: How do I write a resume when changing careers?
q0.answer: Use a hybrid format that leads with transferable skills, write a strong summary explaining your pivot, reframe your work history in the language of your target industry, and add any new credentials.

q1.question: Should I explain my career change in my resume?
q1.answer: Address it briefly in your professional summary. A well-written 2-3 sentence statement that connects your past to your future can be very compelling.

q2.question: Do I need new qualifications to change careers?
q2.answer: Not always. But targeted certifications, bootcamps, or volunteer experience in your new field significantly strengthen your case.

q3.question: How do I handle a career change in an interview?

q4.question: Is a cover letter important for career changers?

item0.title: The Essential Sections
item0.description: Contact info, professional summary, work experience, education, and skills are the core of every effective resume.

item1.title: ATS-Ready Structure
item1.description: Learn how to structure your resume to pass automated screening systems before a human ever reads it.

item2.title: Content That Converts
item2.description: The difference between a resume that gets interviews and one that doesn't comes down to how you frame your experience.

section2.title: The Sections of a Professional Resume
section2.subtitle: What to include in each section.

item0.title: Contact & Header
item0.description: Name, phone, professional email, LinkedIn, location (city/state), and portfolio or GitHub link.

item1.title: Professional Summary
item1.description: A powerful snapshot of who you are, what you offer, and what you're looking for. Tailored to each role.

item2.title: Work Experience
item2.description: Reverse-chronological list of roles with company, title, dates, and 3-5 achievement-focused bullet points each.

item3.title: Education & Skills
item3.description: Degree(s), institution, graduation year. Skills section with technical and soft skills relevant to your target role.

section3.title: How to Write a Resume — Step by Step

step0.title: Choose Your Format
step0.description: Select reverse-chronological (most common), functional (skills-based), or hybrid (combination). When in doubt, use chronological.

step1.title: Fill in Your Information
step1.description: Start with contact info, then write your professional summary last (it's easier once the rest is done).

step2.title: Write Achievement-Focused Bullets
step2.description: For each role, write bullets that start with action verbs and include quantified results. 'Increased X by Y%' not 'responsible for X'.

step3.title: Optimize for ATS and Tailor to the Role
step3.description: Add keywords from the job description. Customize your summary and top bullets for each application.

faq.title: Resume Writing FAQ
faq.subtitle: Common questions about writing a professional resume.

q0.question: What should I put at the top of my resume?
q0.answer: Your contact information (name, phone, email, LinkedIn, location) followed by a professional summary or objective statement.

q1.question: How do I write resume bullet points?
q1.answer: Start each bullet with a strong action verb (Led, Built, Increased, Managed). Include what you did, how you did it, and the result — ideally with a number.

q2.question: How do I tailor my resume for a specific job?
q2.answer: Read the job description, identify the key requirements and keywords, then mirror that language in your summary, skills section, and bullet points.

q3.question: Should I include references on my resume?
q3.answer: No. 'References available upon request' is outdated and wastes space. Employers ask for references separately when they need them.

q4.question: How long should my resume be?
q4.answer: One page for most professionals with under 10 years of experience. Two pages for senior professionals. Never more than two pages unless applying for academic or research positions.

section3.title: How to Write a Resume Summary
section3.tip0: Write your summary LAST. Fill in the rest of your resume first — then summarize the best of it in 2-4 sentences.
section3.tip1: Tailor your summary to each role. Mirror keywords from the job description to boost ATS relevance.
section3.tip2: Avoid vague claims: 'hardworking team player' means nothing. Specific skills and achievements do.
section3.tip3: Use numbers when possible: '8 years of experience', '3x revenue growth', '$2M portfolio managed'.
section3.tip4: Your summary should answer: who are you, what's your strongest value, and why are you applying?

faq.title: Resume Summary FAQ
faq.subtitle: Questions about writing a professional summary.

q0.question: What's the difference between a resume summary and objective?
q0.answer: A summary describes your experience and what you offer. An objective states what you're looking for. Summaries are preferred for experienced professionals; objectives work for entry-level candidates.

q1.question: How long should a resume summary be?
q1.answer: 2-4 sentences (40-80 words). Enough to communicate your value proposition without taking up too much prime real estate at the top of your resume.

q2.question: Should I always include a resume summary?
q2.answer: For most professionals, yes. The only exception is very early-career candidates who have little to summarize — they may prefer a brief objective statement instead.

q3.question: Can I copy resume summary examples?
q3.answer: Use them as inspiration, not copy-paste. A good summary is specific to YOUR experience. Customize any example with your actual achievements, years of experience, and target role.

q4.question: Do ATS systems read the resume summary?
q4.answer: Yes. ATS scans your entire resume including the summary for keywords. Make sure your summary contains the most important keywords from your target job description.

q0.question: How do I make an internship resume with no experience?

q1.question: Should I include my GPA on an internship resume?

q2.question: How long should an internship resume be?

q3.question: What do companies look for in internship resumes?

q4.question: Should I tailor my internship resume for each application?

section1.title: Hard Skills vs Soft Skills — What to Include
section1.subtitle: Understanding what belongs in your skills section.

item0.title: Hard Skills
item0.description: Technical, measurable abilities: programming languages, software tools, certifications, methodologies. Always include these.

item1.title: Soft Skills
item1.description: Interpersonal abilities: communication, leadership, problem-solving. Include selectively — show them through achievements instead.

item2.title: ATS Keyword Matching
item2.description: Mirror skills directly from the job description. ATS scores your resume by how many required skills you list.

section3.title: Resume Skills Section Tips
section3.tip0: Mirror the exact wording from the job description — if they say 'React.js', use 'React.js' not just 'React'.
section3.tip1: Organize skills into categories: Technical Skills, Tools & Software, Languages, Certifications.
section3.tip2: Only list skills you can confidently discuss in an interview. Don't pad your list.
section3.tip3: For technical roles, consider adding proficiency levels: Expert, Proficient, Familiar.
section3.tip4: Update your skills section for every application — different roles prioritize different skills.

faq.title: Resume Skills Section FAQ
faq.subtitle: Everything about building a great skills section.

q0.question: How many skills should I list on my resume?
q0.answer: 10-15 relevant skills is optimal. Too few looks sparse; too many looks like keyword stuffing. Focus on quality and relevance.

q1.question: Should soft skills be on a resume?
q1.answer: Use soft skills sparingly. Instead of just listing 'leadership', show it in your work experience bullets: 'Led a team of 8 engineers to deliver X'.

q2.question: Where should the skills section go on a resume?
q2.answer: For most resumes, after work experience. For career changers or candidates with limited experience, placing it higher (after the summary) can be effective.

q3.question: Should I rate my skill proficiency on my resume?
q3.answer: Avoid visual skill bars or ratings — they're unreliable and ATS can't read them. Instead, use written levels (Proficient, Familiar) or let your experience speak for itself.

q4.question: How do I know which skills to include for a specific job?
q4.answer: Read the job description carefully. Highlight every skill mentioned, then check which ones you have. Include those — they're exactly what the ATS is looking for.

finalCta.title: Build a Skills Section That Gets You Past ATS
finalCta.description: Create a resume with a perfectly structured skills section that matches what recruiters and ATS are looking for in your target role.

q0.question: What is the ideal structure for a cover letter?
q0.answer: A cover letter has 3 parts: 1) Opening — why this role interests you; 2) Body — what you bring to the company (skills + experience + value); 3) Closing — professional sign-off and call to action.

q1.question: How long should a cover letter be?
q1.answer: One page maximum — typically 250-400 words. Recruiters rarely read beyond one page. Be concise, specific to the role, and make every sentence count.

q2.question: Should my cover letter repeat what's already in my resume?
q2.answer: No. The cover letter complements the resume, not repeats it. Use it to explain your motivation, add context to your experience, and show your personality and company knowledge.

q3.question: How do I start a cover letter without 'I am writing to...'?
q3.answer: Start with a strong hook: a relevant achievement, a specific challenge the company faces that you can solve, or genuine enthusiasm for the exact role and mission.

q4.question: Are cover letters still necessary in 2026?
q4.answer: Yes, especially for competitive roles and cold applications. About 40% of recruiters read them consistently, and a strong cover letter can compensate for a less-than-perfect resume.

faq.title: Technician Resume FAQ
faq.subtitle: Technical industry resume questions answered.

q0.question: What certifications should a technician include on their resume?
q0.answer: EPA 608 for HVAC/refrigeration, OSHA 10 or OSHA 30, forklift operator license, manufacturer certifications (Siemens, Allen-Bradley, Schneider), and any relevant trade licenses for your specialty.

q1.question: How should I list technical skills on a technician resume?
q1.answer: Organize by category: Electrical (PLC, motor controls), Mechanical (hydraulics, pneumatics), Diagnostic (oscilloscopes, thermal cameras), and Software (SAP PM, Maximo, AutoCAD).

q2.question: Should I mention CMMS experience on my technician resume?
q2.answer: Yes — CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management Systems) experience is highly valued. List the systems you've used: SAP PM, IBM Maximo, Infor EAM, UpKeep, Fiix, or Limble.

q3.question: How do I show on-call availability on a technician resume?
q3.answer: State it clearly: 'Available for 24/7 on-call rotations', 'On-call one week per month', 'Maintained 97% equipment uptime through rapid response'. Availability is highly valued in maintenance roles.

q4.question: Should I include travel availability on a field service technician resume?
q4.answer: Absolutely. For field service and travelling roles, state: 'Willing to travel up to 80%', 'Regional territory: Southeast', 'Valid driver's license + clean MVR'. Employers want clear expectations set upfront.

faq.title: Pharmacist Resume FAQ
faq.subtitle: Pharmacy-specific resume questions answered.

q0.question: What credentials should a pharmacist include on their resume?
q0.answer: PharmD degree, state pharmacy license (with license number), DEA registration, Board of Pharmacy Specialties (BPS) certifications, and NAPLEX/MPJE pass dates for new graduates.

q1.question: What pharmacy software should I list on my resume?
q1.answer: Retail: Rx30, QS/1, ScriptPro. Hospital: Epic Willow, Cerner FirstNet, Pyxis, Omnicell. PBM: Magellan, Express Scripts. List systems by setting and your proficiency level.

q2.question: Should I include MTM (Medication Therapy Management) on my resume?
q2.answer: Yes — MTM services, comprehensive medication reviews, and patient counseling are valued in retail and clinical pharmacy. Quantify: 'Conducted 50+ MTM consultations per month'.

q3.question: How do I describe patient counseling experience on a pharmacist resume?
q3.answer: Be specific: 'Counseled patients on 50+ new prescriptions daily', 'Provided diabetes management consultations', 'Led smoking cessation program with 30% success rate'.

q4.question: How do I transition from retail to hospital pharmacy on my resume?
q4.answer: Highlight transferable skills: drug knowledge, patient counseling, clinical rotations from pharmacy school, any ASHP residency, and clinical certifications like BCPS or BCPP.
